flashcards.back: The F-distribution is a continuous probability distribution defined as the ratio of two independent chi-square random variables each divided by their respective degrees of freedom: F = (X1/d1) / (X2/d2), where X1 ~ chi-square(d1) and X2 ~ chi-square(d2).

flashcards.front: What is the mean of an F-distribution with denominator degrees of freedom d2?
flashcards.back: E(F) = d2 / (d2 - 2), valid only when d2 > 2. Note that the mean depends only on the denominator degrees of freedom and is always slightly greater than 1.

flashcards.front: How is the F-test statistic computed when comparing two population variances?
flashcards.back: F = s1^2 / s2^2, where s1^2 is the larger sample variance (numerator) and s2^2 is the smaller sample variance (denominator). The degrees of freedom are d1 = n1 - 1 and d2 = n2 - 1.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between the t-distribution and the F-distribution?
flashcards.back: If T follows a t-distribution with n degrees of freedom, then T^2 follows an F-distribution with 1 and n degrees of freedom: T ~ t(n) implies T^2 ~ F(1, n).

flashcards.front: What is the reciprocal property of the F-distribution?
flashcards.back: If F ~ F(d1, d2), then 1/F ~ F(d2, d1). The reciprocal of an F-distributed variable is also F-distributed with the numerator and denominator degrees of freedom swapped.

flashcards.front: In ANOVA, what does the F-statistic represent?
flashcards.back: F = MSB / MSW (Mean Square Between groups divided by Mean Square Within groups). A large F-value indicates that the variability between group means is significantly greater than the variability within groups, suggesting at least one group mean differs.
